questions.question: Under IRC §1202, gain from the sale of qualified small business stock (QSBS) held for more than 5 years may be:
options: Taxed at long-term capital gains rates
Excluded from gross income up to $10 million or 10 times the adjusted basis
questions.explanation: IRC §1202 allows noncorporate taxpayers to exclude up to 100% of gain from the sale of qualified small business stock (QSBS) if held for more than 5 years. The exclusion is limited to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the adjusted basis in the stock. The stock must be in a domestic C corporation with gross assets not exceeding $50 million.

questions.question: For a special allocation of partnership income or loss to be respected by the IRS, the allocation must:
options: Be approved by all partners unanimously
Have substantial economic effect under IRC §704(b)
Allocate at least 50% to the managing partner
Be fixed for the life of the partnership
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(b), a partner's distributive share is determined by the partnership agreement, but allocations must have substantial economic effect to be respected. This requires that the allocation affect the dollar amount of the partners' shares of partnership economic gain or loss independent of tax consequences (i.e., the allocation must be reflected in capital accounts and followed in liquidation).

questions.question: Which of the following items must be separately stated on Schedule K of Form 1120-S?

questions.question: For taxpayers above the income threshold, the Section 199A QBI deduction for a non-SSTB is limited to the greater of: 50% of W-2 wages, or 25% of W-2 wages plus:

questions.question: Under MACRS, most tangible personal property placed in service during the year uses which depreciation convention?
questions.explanation: Under MACRS, the half-year convention applies to most tangible personal property (equipment, furniture, vehicles, etc.) placed in service during the year. This convention treats the property as placed in service at the midpoint of the year, allowing a half-year of depreciation in the first and last years. The mid-quarter convention applies instead if more than 40% of property is placed in service in the last quarter.

questions.question: For property placed in service in 2024, the first-year bonus depreciation percentage under IRC §168(k) is:

questions.question: If listed property (such as a vehicle used partly for personal purposes) is not used more than 50% for business, the taxpayer must:
options: Use the straight-line method over the ADS recovery period
Expense the full cost under Section 179
questions.explanation: If listed property is not used more than 50% for qualified business use, the taxpayer must depreciate it using the straight-line method over the alternative depreciation system (ADS) recovery period. Section 179 expensing and bonus depreciation are not available. If business use drops below 50% in a subsequent year, excess depreciation must be recaptured.

questions.question: The general business credit under IRC §38 is limited to the taxpayer's net income tax minus the greater of the tentative minimum tax or:
options: 25% of net regular tax liability over $25,000
questions.explanation: The general business credit is limited to the taxpayer's net income tax minus the greater of the tentative minimum tax or 25% of net regular tax liability exceeding $25,000. Unused credits may be carried back 1 year and forward 20 years. The general business credit is a combination of multiple credits including the investment credit, work opportunity credit, and research credit.

questions.question: Under the cash method of accounting, income is recognized when:
options: Earned, regardless of when payment is received
questions.explanation: Under the cash method, income is recognized when actually or constructively received. Constructive receipt occurs when income is available to the taxpayer without substantial limitation (e.g., a check received in December but not cashed until January). Expenses are deducted when paid. This contrasts with the accrual method, which recognizes income when earned and expenses when incurred.

questions.question: Which type of business is generally required to use the accrual method of accounting?
options: A sole proprietorship with $5 million in gross receipts
A C corporation with average annual gross receipts exceeding $30 million
Any partnership with more than 2 partners
questions.explanation: Under IRC §448, C corporations (and partnerships with a C corporation partner) with average annual gross receipts exceeding $30 million (adjusted for inflation from the $25M TCJA base) are generally required to use the accrual method. The gross receipts test uses the average of the prior 3 tax years. Exceptions exist for farming businesses and qualified personal service corporations.

questions.question: Which entity type provides the BEST ability for an owner-employee to receive tax-free fringe benefits (such as employer-paid health insurance)?
questions.explanation: C corporations provide the best advantage for tax-free fringe benefits to owner-employees. The corporation deducts the cost, and the shareholder-employee does not recognize income. In contrast, S corporation shareholders owning more than 2% and partners must include the value of most fringe benefits in income (though they may deduct health insurance above the line).

questions.question: A net Section 1231 gain is treated as:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1231, if Section 1231 gains exceed Section 1231 losses for the year, the net gain is treated as long-term capital gain, eligible for preferential tax rates. However, if Section 1231 losses exceed gains, the net loss is treated as an ordinary loss. This is the "best of both worlds" rule. The 5-year lookback rule may recharacterize some gain as ordinary.

questions.question: Under current tax law, a partnership terminates for tax purposes when:
options: 50% or more of partnership interests are sold within 12 months
No part of the business continues to be carried on by any partner
The partnership has a net loss for 3 consecutive years
A new partner is admitted to the partnership
questions.explanation: Under current law (post-TCJA, effective for tax years beginning after 2017), a partnership terminates for tax purposes only when no part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any partner. The TCJA repealed the technical termination rule that existed under former IRC §708(b)(1)(B) (50% sale within 12 months).

questions.question: An S corporation with accumulated earnings and profits from C corporation years may have its S election terminated if passive investment income exceeds what percentage of gross receipts for 3 consecutive years?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(3), if an S corporation has accumulated earnings and profits from prior C corporation years and its passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive years, the S election is automatically terminated. Additionally, under IRC §1375, a tax is imposed on excess net passive income in each year the 25% threshold is exceeded. This can be avoided by distributing the accumulated E&P.

questions.question: When a partner's interest in a partnership changes during the year, the partner's share of partnership income may be determined using:
options: The interim closing or proration method, as elected by the partnership
Only the percentage held on December 31
A simple average of beginning and ending percentages
questions.explanation: Under IRC §706(d), when a partner's interest varies during the year (e.g., due to a sale or new partner admission), the partnership may use either the interim closing of the books method (closing the books on the date of change) or the proration method (prorating items daily). The partnership agreement or the partners may agree on which method to use.

questions.question: Under IRC §1245, when depreciable personal property is sold at a gain, the depreciation previously taken is recaptured as:
options: Capital gain up to the amount of depreciation
Ordinary income up to the amount of depreciation
Passive income subject to the 3.8% NIIT
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1245, gain on the sale of depreciable personal property (Section 1245 property) is recaptured as ordinary income to the extent of depreciation previously taken. Any gain in excess of the depreciation amount is treated as Section 1231 gain (potentially long-term capital gain). This prevents taxpayers from converting ordinary deductions into capital gains.

questions.question: When a corporation is thinly capitalized (excess debt relative to equity), the IRS may recharacterize debt as:
options: Equity, resulting in interest payments being treated as nondeductible dividends
Accumulated earnings subject to penalty tax
questions.explanation: When a corporation has an unusually high debt-to-equity ratio (thin capitalization), the IRS may recharacterize the purported debt as equity under IRC §385. The consequence is that interest payments become nondeductible (treated as dividend distributions), and repayments are treated as returns of capital rather than debt repayment. Factors considered include the debt-to-equity ratio, whether the debt is held proportionate to stock ownership, and whether there is a fixed repayment schedule.

Against the alternative minimum tax only

questions.question: When a business changes its accounting method with IRS consent, the Section 481(a) adjustment:
options: Is always recognized in full in the year of change
Prevents the business from duplicating or omitting income or deductions due to the change
questions.explanation: The IRC §481(a) adjustment is a cumulative catch-up amount that prevents items of income or deduction from being duplicated or omitted as a result of the accounting method change. A positive adjustment (income) is generally spread over 4 years; a negative adjustment (deduction) is taken entirely in the year of change. The adjustment ensures the lifetime income is correct.

questions.question: In a partnership, deductions attributable to nonrecourse liabilities are generally allocated to:
options: Partners in accordance with their share of partnership profits
The partner who guarantees the debt
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.704-2, nonrecourse deductions are deductions funded by nonrecourse liabilities (where no partner bears the economic risk of loss). These deductions must be allocated in accordance with the partners' interests in partnership profits (or any other manner consistent with the regulations). Since no partner bears the loss risk, the allocation follows profit-sharing ratios.

questions.question: Under IRC §267, losses on sales between related parties are:

questions.explanation: Under IRC §267(a)(1), no deduction is allowed for losses on sales or exchanges of property between related parties. questions.question: A partnership must generally adopt the same tax year as its:
options: Partners owning a majority interest (majority interest tax year)
questions.explanation: Under IRC §706(b), a partnership must generally use the tax year of its majority-interest partners (partners owning more than 50% of profits and capital). If there is no majority interest tax year, the partnership must use the tax year of all principal partners. If neither rule determines a year, the partnership must use the year that produces the least aggregate deferral. A Section 444 election may allow a different year.

questions.question: Which of the following factors most strongly indicates that a worker is an employee rather than an independent contractor?
options: The worker uses their own tools and equipment
The business controls when, where, and how the work is performed
The worker sets their own schedule
The worker provides services to multiple clients
questions.explanation: The IRS uses common-law rules to determine worker classification, focusing on the degree of control and independence. Behavioral control (when, where, and how work is performed) is a primary factor. Other factors include financial control (how the worker is paid, expense reimbursement) and the type of relationship (written contracts, benefits). Workers who control their own methods are more likely independent contractors.

questions.question: The trust fund recovery penalty under IRC §6672 may be assessed against:

Only the CEO of the corporation
All tax return preparers who file the return

questions.question: The Federal Unemployment Tax Act (FUTA) tax rate is applied to the first how many dollars of each employee's wages per year?
questions.explanation: The FUTA tax rate of 6.0% is applied to the first $7,000 of wages paid to each employee per calendar year. However, employers receive a credit of up to 5.4% for state unemployment taxes paid, resulting in an effective FUTA rate of 0.6% ($42 per employee per year). FUTA is paid entirely by the employer; it is not withheld from employee wages.

questions.question: Section 382 limits the use of a corporation's net operating losses (NOLs) following:
options: Any change in the corporation's fiscal year
An ownership change of more than 50 percentage points
A change in the corporation's accounting method
The appointment of a new CEO
questions.explanation: IRC §382 limits the amount of pre-change NOLs that can be used annually after an ownership change. An ownership change occurs when the percentage of stock owned by 5% shareholders increases by more than 50 percentage points over a 3-year testing period. The annual limitation equals the value of the loss corporation multiplied by the long-term tax-exempt rate. This prevents trafficking in NOL carryforwards.

questions.question: Under the disguised sale rules of IRC §707(a)(2)(B), a contribution of property followed by a related distribution from the partnership may be recharacterized as:
options: A sale of the property to the partnership
A gift to the other partners
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) and Treas. Reg. §1.707-3, if a partner contributes property and receives a distribution from the partnership within 2 years, the transactions may be presumed to be a disguised sale. questions.question: Which of the following costs is a deductible/amortizable organization expense for a partnership?
options: Commissions paid for selling partnership interests
Legal fees for negotiating the partnership agreement
Costs of printing and selling partnership interests to investors
Advertising expenses related to selling limited partnership interests
questions.explanation: Under IRC §709(b), partnership organizational expenses (such as legal fees for preparing the partnership agreement, filing fees, and accounting fees for setting up the partnership) may be deducted up to $5,000 and the remainder amortized over 180 months. questions.question: In what order are adjustments made to a partner's basis in their partnership interest at year-end?
options: Increase for income items, decrease for distributions, decrease for losses and deductions
Decrease for distributions first, then increase for income
The order does not matter as all adjustments are simultaneous
Decrease for losses first, then increase for income, then decrease for distributions
questions.explanation: Under IRC §705, the basis ordering rules require: (1) First, increase basis for all income items (ordinary and separately stated, including tax-exempt income); (2) Then decrease basis for distributions; (3) Then decrease basis for nondeductible, noncapital expenditures; (4) Finally, decrease basis for losses and deductions. This ordering ensures that distributions and losses are properly limited.

options: Next day after the accumulation exceeds $100,000
Last day of the same month
Last day of the following quarter
questions.explanation: Under the monthly deposit rule, an employer must deposit employment taxes accumulated during a calendar month by the 15th day of the following month. The semi-weekly deposit rule requires deposits within 3 banking days for larger employers. The $100,000 next-day deposit rule applies when undeposited taxes reach $100,000 or more on any day, regardless of the employer's usual schedule.

questions.question: For employment tax purposes, a single-member LLC that is a disregarded entity:
options: Reports employment taxes under its own EIN and is treated as a separate entity
Must use the owner's SSN for all employment tax returns
questions.explanation: While a single-member LLC is disregarded for income tax purposes, it is treated as a separate entity for employment tax and certain excise tax purposes. questions.question: A closely held C corporation (not a PSC) that has passive activity losses may offset those losses against:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §469(e)(2), a closely held C corporation (other than a PSC) may use passive activity losses to offset active business income but not portfolio income. This is more favorable than the rules for individuals and PSCs, which cannot offset passive losses against either active or portfolio income. A closely held corporation is one in which 5 or fewer individuals own more than 50% of the stock.

questions.question: A partner contributes property with a basis of $10,000 and FMV of $50,000, subject to a $30,000 mortgage, to a partnership where the partner receives a 20% interest. After the contribution, the partner's share of the liability is $6,000. What is the partner's outside basis?
questions.explanation: Initial basis from property contribution: $10,000. The partner is relieved of $30,000 of liability (treated as a deemed distribution). The partner's share of the partnership liability is $6,000 (20% × $30,000) (treated as a deemed contribution). Net effect: $10,000 - $30,000 + $6,000 = -$14,000. Since basis cannot go below zero, the partner recognizes $14,000 of gain and has a $0 basis.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about partnership formation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about partnership formation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is CORRECT - Initial basis from property contribution: $10,000.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about partnership formation.

questions.question: Section 530 of the Revenue Act of 1978 provides relief to employers for worker misclassification if the employer:
options: Had a reasonable basis for treating workers as independent contractors and filed consistent information returns
Classified all workers as employees going forward
Paid the workers above minimum wage
questions.explanation: Section 530 provides relief from federal employment taxes if the employer (1) had a reasonable basis for treating workers as independent contractors (such as judicial precedent, IRS ruling, prior audit, or industry practice), (2) treated the workers consistently, and (3) filed all required information returns (Form 1099) for the workers. This is a safe harbor, not a substantive test of worker status.

questions.question: Taxpayers may elect to aggregate multiple qualified trades or businesses for purposes of the Section 199A QBI deduction if:
options: The businesses are in different industries
The businesses share common ownership, are part of the same industry, and meet specific grouping requirements
Each business has fewer than 10 employees
Total combined revenue is under $1 million
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.199A-4, taxpayers may aggregate multiple businesses if they meet certain criteria: (1) the same person or group owns at least 50% of each business, (2) the businesses share at least two of three factors (common management, common business facilities, or significant centralized business elements). Aggregation allows combining W-2 wages and UBIA across businesses for the W-2/UBIA limitation.

questions.question: An affiliated group of corporations may file a consolidated return if the common parent owns directly or indirectly at least what percentage of the voting power and value of each includible corporation?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1504(a), an affiliated group may file a consolidated return if the common parent owns at least 80% of the total voting power and 80% of the total value of the stock of each includible corporation. S corporations, tax-exempt organizations, foreign corporations, and certain other entities are not includible corporations. A consolidated return allows offsetting profits and losses among group members.

questions.question: Health insurance premiums paid by an S corporation on behalf of a more-than-2% shareholder-employee are:
options: Excluded from the shareholder's income as a tax-free fringe benefit
Included in the shareholder's W-2 wages but not subject to FICA taxes
Deductible by the S corporation but taxable as a guaranteed payment
Nondeductible by both the corporation and the shareholder
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1372, a more-than-2% S corporation shareholder is treated like a partner for fringe benefit purposes. Health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation must be included in the shareholder-employee's W-2 wages (Box 1) but are generally not subject to FICA taxes. The shareholder may then deduct the premiums as a self-employed health insurance deduction (above-the-line) on their personal return.

An automatic 6-month extension with no additional filing

questions.question: Under IRC §163(j), the deduction for business interest expense is generally limited to the sum of business interest income plus:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §163(j), as amended by the TCJA, the deduction for business interest expense is limited to business interest income plus 30% of adjusted taxable income (ATI). For tax years beginning after 2021, ATI is computed without adding back depreciation, amortization, and depletion. Small businesses (average gross receipts of $30 million or less) are generally exempt from this limitation.

questions.question: A new business incurs $42,000 of startup costs. Under IRC §195, how much may be deducted in the first year?
options: $5,000 immediately plus amortization of the remainder over 180 months
$0 — all startup costs must be capitalized
questions.explanation: Under IRC §195, a taxpayer may deduct up to $5,000 of startup costs in the year the business begins operations. The $5,000 deduction is reduced dollar-for-dollar for startup costs exceeding $50,000. Since $42,000 is under $50,000, the full $5,000 immediate deduction is available. The remaining $37,000 is amortized over 180 months beginning in the month active business starts.

questions.question: For a self-employed individual, the maximum SEP-IRA contribution is the lesser of $69,000 (2024) or:
questions.explanation: The maximum SEP-IRA contribution is the lesser of $69,000 (2024, indexed for inflation) or 25% of the employee's compensation. questions.question: A SIMPLE IRA plan may be established by an employer with how many or fewer employees?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §408(p), a SIMPLE (Savings Incentive Match Plan for Employees) IRA plan may be maintained by employers with 100 or fewer employees who received at least $5,000 in compensation during the preceding year. options: Regularly and exclusively as the principal place of business
At least 20 hours per week for business
Only if no other office space is available

questions.question: A business has $100,000 of debt cancelled. The business is solvent at the time of cancellation. How is the cancelled debt treated?
options: It is excluded from income as a gift
It is included in gross income as cancellation of debt income
It is deductible as a business loss
It reduces the basis of business assets
questions.explanation: Under IRC §61(a)(11), cancellation of debt (COD) income is generally included in gross income. Exceptions exist for bankruptcy (Title 11 case), insolvency, qualified farm indebtedness, qualified real property business indebtedness, and qualified principal residence indebtedness. Since the business is solvent, the full $100,000 is included in income.

questions.question: A business using the accrual method that previously included an amount in income but cannot collect the receivable may take a deduction for:
options: A personal bad debt treated as a short-term capital loss
A business bad debt deduction as an ordinary loss
A casualty loss on Schedule A
questions.explanation: Under IRC §166, a business bad debt that becomes wholly or partially worthless is deductible as an ordinary loss. For accrual-method taxpayers, the amount previously included in income that becomes uncollectible can be deducted. Business bad debts are fully deductible as ordinary losses, while nonbusiness bad debts are deductible only as short-term capital losses.

questions.question: Two or more corporations forming a parent-subsidiary controlled group must share a single:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1561, component members of a controlled group of corporations must share certain tax benefits, including the accumulated earnings credit ($250,000) and the Section 179 deduction. A parent-subsidiary controlled group exists when a common parent owns at least 80% of one or more subsidiary corporations. This prevents taxpayers from multiplying tax benefits by creating multiple corporations.

questions.question: Under IRC §183, an activity is presumed to be engaged in for profit if it produces a profit in at least how many of the last 5 tax years?
questions.explanation: Under IRC §183(d), an activity is presumed to be engaged in for profit if it produces income in at least 3 out of the last 5 consecutive tax years ending with the current year (2 out of 7 years for horse breeding, training, showing, or racing). questions.question: Under the installment method of IRC §453, gain from a qualifying sale is recognized:
options: Entirely in the year of sale
As payments are received, based on the gross profit percentage
Only when the final payment is received
questions.explanation: Under the installment method, gain is recognized proportionally as payments are received. The gross profit percentage (total gain divided by total contract price) is applied to each payment to determine the taxable portion. Dealers in personal property and sales of publicly traded property are generally ineligible. The installment method applies automatically unless the taxpayer elects out.

questions.question: In a defined benefit retirement plan, the employer's contribution is based on:
options: A fixed percentage of each employee's compensation
An actuarial calculation of the amount needed to fund promised future benefits
Employee elective deferrals matched by the employer
questions.explanation: In a defined benefit plan, the employer promises a specific benefit at retirement (typically based on salary and years of service). The employer's annual contribution is determined by an actuary based on assumptions about investment returns, employee turnover, mortality, and other factors. The annual benefit limit is $275,000 (2024). This contrasts with defined contribution plans where the contribution is fixed.

questions.question: Income from bartering (exchanging goods or services) is:
options: Not taxable because no cash is received
Taxable at the fair market value of the goods or services received
Taxable only if reported on Form 1099-B
questions.explanation: The fair market value of goods or services received in a barter exchange must be included in gross income in the year received. This is true even though no cash changes hands. questions.question: A corporation makes a $200,000 interest-free loan to its controlling shareholder. Under IRC §7872, the IRS treats this transaction as:
options: A tax-free advance with no consequences
The corporation making a distribution (dividend) to the shareholder equal to the forgone interest, and the shareholder paying interest back to the corporation
A capital contribution from the shareholder
questions.explanation: Under IRC §7872, below-market loans between a corporation and its shareholder are recharacterized. The corporation is treated as paying a distribution (dividend) to the shareholder equal to the forgone interest (using the applicable federal rate), and the shareholder is treated as paying interest to the corporation. The corporation includes the deemed interest as income, and the shareholder may or may not be able to deduct it.

questions.question: The 3.8% net investment income tax (NIIT) under IRC §1411 applies to:
options: All business income regardless of participation
Net investment income of individuals with modified AGI exceeding $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ)
Only capital gains from stock sales
questions.explanation: The 3.8% NIIT applies to the lesser of (1) net investment income or (2) the excess of MAGI over $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ). It does not include wages, self-employment income, Social Security benefits, or income from actively conducted businesses.

questions.question: Prior to the TCJA, a partnership could experience a technical termination if 50% or more of interests were sold within 12 months. After TCJA, technical terminations:
options: Apply only to partnerships with more than 100 partners
Were repealed — partnerships terminate only when business operations cease
Were replaced by mandatory dissolution rules
questions.explanation: The TCJA repealed the technical termination rule under former IRC §708(b)(1)(B), effective for tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. A partnership now terminates only when no part of any business, financial operation, or venture continues to be carried on by any partner in a partnership. This simplification eliminated the need to close the books and restart depreciation on a technical termination.

questions.question: Under IRC §197, acquired intangible assets such as goodwill, workforce in place, and customer-based intangibles are amortized over:

questions.question: For an accrual-method taxpayer, a deduction for a business expense generally cannot be taken until:
options: All events have occurred to establish the liability and economic performance has occurred
The expense is paid in cash
The end of the tax year
questions.explanation: Under IRC §461(h), an accrual-method taxpayer may deduct an expense only when (1) all events have occurred to establish the fact and amount of the liability (the all-events test) AND (2) economic performance has occurred. For services and property received, economic performance occurs when the services or property are provided. For recurring items, an exception allows deduction if economic performance occurs within 8.5 months.

questions.question: An individual who actively participates in rental real estate activities may deduct up to how much in rental losses against nonpassive income?

questions.question: In a Section 1031 like-kind exchange, a taxpayer receives replacement real property plus $20,000 cash. The taxpayer's realized gain on the exchange is $50,000. How much gain must be recognized?
questions.explanation: In a like-kind exchange where boot (cash or non-like-kind property) is received, gain is recognized to the lesser of (1) the realized gain ($50,000) or (2) the boot received ($20,000). Since the boot ($20,000) is less than the realized gain ($50,000), the taxpayer recognizes $20,000 of gain. The remaining $30,000 of gain is deferred.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about business income.

whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about business income.

questions.question: In a deferred like-kind exchange under IRC §1031, the replacement property must be identified within:
questions.explanation: Under the deferred exchange rules, the replacement property must be identified in writing within 45 days after the transfer of the relinquished property. The exchange must be completed within the earlier of 180 days after the transfer or the due date (including extensions) of the taxpayer's return for the year of the transfer. These deadlines are strict and cannot be extended.

questions.question: In a Type A (statutory merger) corporate reorganization under IRC §368(a)(1)(A), the shareholders of the target corporation generally:
options: Recognize all gain on the exchange of stock
Recognize no gain or loss if they receive only stock of the acquiring corporation
Must include the fair market value of new stock in income
Lose their basis in the stock
questions.explanation: Under IRC §354, shareholders who exchange stock of the target corporation solely for stock of the acquiring corporation in a qualifying Type A reorganization (statutory merger) recognize no gain or loss. If boot (cash or other property) is also received, gain is recognized to the extent of boot under IRC §356. The shareholders take a substituted basis in the new stock.

questions.question: The uniform capitalization (UNICAP) rules under IRC §263A require certain taxpayers to:
options: Expense all production costs in the year incurred
Capitalize direct and indirect costs allocable to produced or acquired property
Use the cash method for all manufacturing costs
questions.explanation: Under IRC §263A, taxpayers who produce property or acquire property for resale must capitalize both direct costs (materials, labor) and certain indirect costs (rent, depreciation, utilities) allocable to that property. These costs become part of inventory or the asset's basis. Exceptions exist for small taxpayers meeting the $30 million gross receipts test, freelance authors/artists, and certain farming activities.

questions.question: Under IRC §743, a mandatory basis adjustment to partnership assets is required (without a Section 754 election) when a partnership has a substantial built-in loss, meaning the partnership's adjusted basis in its assets exceeds FMV by more than:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §743(d), a mandatory basis adjustment under IRC §743(b) is required upon a transfer of a partnership interest if the partnership has a substantial built-in loss, defined as the partnership's aggregate adjusted basis in its property exceeding the FMV of such property by more than $250,000. This rule was added to prevent loss trafficking without a Section 754 election.

questions.question: Under the passive activity grouping rules, a taxpayer may group multiple business activities as a single activity if the activities form:
options: An appropriate economic unit based on facts and circumstances
Only activities in the same SIC code
Activities owned by the same entity type
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.469-4, taxpayers may group activities as a single activity if they form an appropriate economic unit for measuring gain or loss. Factors include (1) similarities and differences in types of business, (2) extent of common control, (3) extent of common ownership, (4) geographical location, and (5) interdependencies among activities. Once grouped, the grouping generally cannot be changed unless the original grouping was clearly inappropriate.

questions.question: For an S corporation with accumulated earnings and profits (AE&P) from prior C corporation years, distributions are first treated as:
options: Tax-free return of income from AAA (accumulated adjustments account)
Return of capital reducing stock basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1368(c), when an S corporation has accumulated E&P, distributions are applied in this order: (1) tax-free from the accumulated adjustments account (AAA) — representing previously taxed S corp income, (2) dividends from accumulated E&P, (3) tax-free return of remaining basis in stock, and (4) gain from sale of stock (to the extent exceeding basis). The S corporation may elect to distribute E&P first.

whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation operations.

questions.question: An S corporation may elect to treat a wholly owned domestic subsidiary as a qualified Subchapter S subsidiary (QSub). What is the tax treatment of a QSub?
options: It files a separate return as a C corporation
It is treated as a disregarded entity — all items flow to the parent S corporation
It must make a separate S election
It is treated as a partnership with the parent
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1361(b)(3), an S corporation may elect to treat a 100%-owned domestic subsidiary as a QSub. The QSub is not treated as a separate corporation for federal tax purposes (it is a disregarded entity). All assets, liabilities, income, and deductions are treated as belonging to the parent S corporation. The QSub does not file a separate return.

questions.question: Under the luxury automobile depreciation limitations of IRC §280F, the maximum first-year depreciation deduction for a passenger automobile placed in service in 2024 (not electing bonus depreciation) is approximately:
questions.explanation: Under IRC §280F, passenger automobiles are subject to annual depreciation limits (luxury auto limits). For vehicles placed in service in 2024, the first-year limit is approximately $12,400 (without bonus depreciation). With first-year bonus depreciation, the limit increases to approximately $20,400. These limits are designed to prevent taxpayers from writing off luxury vehicles quickly.

questions.question: Constructive receipt applies when income is:
options: Credited to the taxpayer's account or set aside without substantial restriction
Subject to a future condition precedent
Billed but payment is not due for 90 days
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.451-2, income is constructively received when it is credited to the taxpayer's account, set apart, or otherwise made available so that the taxpayer may draw upon it without substantial limitations or restrictions. Income is not constructively received if the taxpayer's control over receipt is subject to substantial limitations, such as a substantial forfeiture penalty.

questions.question: A C corporation can avoid the estimated tax penalty by paying quarterly installments equal to at least what percentage of the current year's tax liability?
questions.explanation: A corporation can avoid the estimated tax penalty by paying estimated taxes equal to or exceeding 100% of the current year's tax liability (in divided quarterly installments). Alternatively, the corporation can use 100% of the prior year's tax (not available for large corporations for installments after the first). The penalty is based on the underpayment rate for each quarter.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about corporate taxation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - A corporation can avoid the estimated tax penalty by paying estimated taxes equal to or exceeding 100% of the current year's tax liability (in divided quarterly installments).

questions.question: Under IRC §706(d), when a partner's interest is sold or exchanged during the year, the partnership's income from the date of transfer:
options: Is allocated entirely to the selling partner
Must not be allocated to a transferee partner via retroactive allocation to periods before the transfer
Is allocated equally among all partners
Is excluded from the partnership return
questions.explanation: IRC §706(d)(1) prohibits retroactive allocations — partnership items cannot be allocated to a time before a partner obtained their interest. When a partner sells their interest midyear, the partnership must determine each partner's share of items by either an interim closing of the books or a daily proration method. The buying partner receives income only for the period after the purchase.

questions.question: Nonresidential real property is depreciated under MACRS over:
questions.explanation: Under MACRS, nonresidential real property (commercial buildings, offices, warehouses) is depreciated over 39 years using the straight-line method and the mid-month convention. Residential rental property is depreciated over 27.5 years. The alternative depreciation system (ADS) period is 40 years for nonresidential real property.

questions.question: A taxpayer who invests capital gains into a Qualified Opportunity Zone (QOZ) Fund may:

Deduct the investment as a business expense
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1400Z-2, a taxpayer who invests eligible capital gains into a Qualified Opportunity Zone Fund may defer recognition of the invested gain until the earlier of: (1) the date the QOZ investment is sold or exchanged, or (2) December 31, 2026. questions.question: A series LLC is a type of LLC that allows:
options: Separate series within the LLC, each with its own assets, members, and liability shield
questions.explanation: A series LLC is a form of LLC authorized in some states that allows the creation of separate internal series, each with its own assets, members, managers, and liabilities. The assets of one series are generally protected from the liabilities of other series. For federal tax purposes, each series may be treated as a separate entity for classification purposes under the check-the-box regulations.

questions.question: Under IRC §704(e), a family partnership in which capital is a material income-producing factor requires that:
options: All family members participate equally in management
The donor/seller partner must be allocated reasonable compensation for services before profits are allocated
No partner may be a minor
The partnership must be organized as a limited partnership
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(e)(2), if a family member acquires a partnership interest by gift (or purchase from a family member) and capital is a material income-producing factor, the donor partner must first be allocated reasonable compensation for services rendered. questions.question: Schedule M-1 of Form 1120 reconciles:
options: Net income per books with taxable income
Current and prior year tax liabilities
questions.explanation: Schedule M-1 reconciles the corporation's net income per books (financial accounting income) with taxable income per the return. Common adjustments include: adding back book expenses not deductible for tax (e.g., federal income tax, 50% of meals, penalties), subtracting tax deductions not recorded on books (e.g., depreciation differences), adding taxable income not on books, and subtracting book income not taxable.

questions.question: For a business expense to be deductible under IRC §162, it must be:
options: Ordinary and necessary in carrying on a trade or business
Approved by the IRS in advance
questions.explanation: Under IRC §162(a), a deduction is allowed for all ordinary and necessary expenses paid or incurred during the taxable year in carrying on a trade or business. An "ordinary" expense is one that is common and accepted in the business. A "necessary" expense is one that is helpful and appropriate (not indispensable). The expense must also be reasonable in amount.

questions.question: A key advantage of a partnership over an S corporation is that partnerships:
options: Provide liability protection for all partners
Allow flexible allocation of income, gain, loss, and deductions among partners
Are not subject to self-employment tax
Can have unlimited classes of ownership
questions.explanation: Partnerships can make special allocations of income, gain, loss, and deductions to partners in proportions different from their ownership percentages, as long as the allocations have substantial economic effect under IRC §704(b). S corporations must allocate all items pro rata based on stock ownership (per-share, per-day). This flexibility makes partnerships ideal for complex ownership arrangements.

questions.question: An S corporation shareholder's basis is adjusted annually in what order?
options: Distributions first, then income, then losses
Income and gain items increase basis first, then distributions reduce basis, then losses and deduction items reduce basis
Losses first, then income, then distributions
Distributions first, then losses, then income
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1367 and Treas. Reg. §1.1367-1(f), an S corporation shareholder's stock basis is adjusted annually in the following order: (1) increased for income items (separately and nonseparately stated income); (2) increased for the excess of deductions for depletion over basis of the property; (3) decreased (but not below zero) for nondividend distributions; (4) decreased for nondeductible, noncapital expenses and depletion; (5) decreased for items of loss and deduction (limited to remaining basis).

questions.question: A C corporation that converts to an S corporation may be subject to the built-in gains (BIG) tax under IRC §1374 if it:
options: Has any assets when it converts
Sells or disposes of assets with net unrealized built-in gain within 5 years of the S election, subject to the corporate-rate tax on the recognized built-in gain
Earns any income in the first year
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1374, a C corporation that converts to S status is subject to the built-in gains tax at the corporate rate (currently 21%) on net recognized built-in gain from assets that had appreciation on the date of conversion, if the gain is recognized during the recognition period (5 years from the effective date of the S election). The BIG tax applies only to the excess of gain over the net unrealized built-in gain at conversion. Any BIG tax paid flows through to shareholders as a separately stated item.

questions.question: For a partnership's special allocation of income or loss to be respected by the IRS, the allocation must:
options: Have substantial economic effect as defined under IRC §704(b) and the Treasury Regulations
Be in proportion to capital contributions
Be approved by the IRS in advance
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(b), a partner's share of income, gain, loss, deduction, or credit is determined by the partnership agreement if the allocation has substantial economic effect. The Treas. Reg. §1.704-1(b)(2) requires two parts: (1) economic effect — the allocation must be reflected in partner capital accounts, with liquidating distributions based on capital accounts, and deficit restoration obligations; and (2) substantiality — the allocation must have a meaningful economic impact apart from tax consequences.

questions.question: Guaranteed payments to a partner under IRC §707(c) are:
options: Treated as distributions of partnership profits
Payments for services or capital that are determined without regard to partnership income, deductible by the partnership, and ordinary income to the receiving partner
Not reported on the partner's individual return
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(c), guaranteed payments are payments to a partner for services rendered or for the use of capital that are determined without regard to the income of the partnership. They are deductible by the partnership as a business expense and reported as ordinary income to the receiving partner (not subject to self-employment tax if for the use of capital, but subject to SE tax if for services). Guaranteed payments are reported on Schedule K-1, Box 4.

questions.question: The accumulated earnings tax under IRC §531 is imposed on corporations that:
options: Accumulate earnings and profits beyond the reasonable needs of the business to avoid shareholder-level tax on dividends
Pay dividends in excess of earnings
questions.explanation: Under IRC §531, the accumulated earnings tax (AET) of 20% is imposed on corporations that accumulate earnings and profits (E&P) beyond the reasonable needs of the business for the purpose of avoiding income tax on shareholders. Reasonable business needs include expansion, debt retirement, and working capital. An accumulated earnings credit of $250,000 ($150,000 for personal service corporations) is allowed. The burden of proof is on the IRS if the Notice of Deficiency relies on this provision.

questions.question: A personal holding company (PHC) is a corporation that meets both the stock ownership test and the income test. The income test requires that:
options: At least 50% of the corporation's gross income is from manufacturing
At least 60% of adjusted ordinary gross income consists of PHC income (dividends, interest, rents, royalties, certain personal service contracts)
All income is from active business operations
questions.explanation: Under IRC §542, a corporation is a PHC if: (1) the stock ownership test is met — more than 50% of outstanding stock is owned (directly or constructively) by 5 or fewer individuals at any time during the last half of the tax year; and (2) the income test is met — at least 60% of adjusted ordinary gross income is PHC income (dividends, interest, rents below certain thresholds, royalties, and income from personal service contracts). PHCs face a 20% penalty tax on undistributed PHC income.

questions.question: Under IRC §751, "hot assets" in a partnership include:
options: Unrealized receivables and inventory items, which convert capital gain to ordinary income on sale or exchange of a partnership interest
questions.explanation: Under IRC §751, hot assets include unrealized receivables (including certain recapture items under IRC §1245 and §1250) and inventory items (substantially appreciated or not). questions.question: Compared to a sole proprietorship, an S corporation may reduce self-employment taxes because:
options: S corporation income is completely exempt from all payroll taxes
Only the shareholder-employee's reasonable salary is subject to FICA; the remaining profit distributions are not subject to self-employment or FICA tax
S corporations never pay employment taxes
The S corporation pays all employment taxes at a lower rate
questions.explanation: In a sole proprietorship, all net earnings are subject to self-employment tax. In an S corporation, only the shareholder-employee's reasonable salary (W-2 wages) is subject to FICA taxes. Distributions of remaining profits to the shareholder are not subject to self-employment or FICA tax. However, the IRS requires that the salary be "reasonable" — setting an unreasonably low salary to avoid FICA taxes may result in recharacterization of distributions as wages.

questions.question: The Accumulated Adjustments Account (AAA) of an S corporation:
options: Tracks the corporation's retained earnings since inception
Tracks the S corporation's post-1982 undistributed income that has been taxed to shareholders but not yet distributed, adjusting for income, losses, deductions, and nondividend distributions
Is the same as the shareholder's stock basis
questions.explanation: The AAA is a corporate-level account that tracks the cumulative total of undistributed net income of the S corporation since 1983 (or since the S election became effective). It is increased by income items and decreased by losses, deductions, nondeductible expenses, and distributions. The AAA is important for S corporations with accumulated E&P from prior C corporation years — distributions from AAA are tax-free (return of previously taxed income), while distributions exceeding AAA from E&P are taxed as dividends.

questions.question: A C corporation's charitable contribution deduction is limited to:

There is no limit on corporate charitable deductions
questions.explanation: Under IRC §170(b)(2), a C corporation's charitable contribution deduction is limited to 10% of its taxable income, computed without regard to the charitable contribution deduction, the dividends received deduction (DRD), any net operating loss carryback, and any capital loss carryback. Contributions exceeding the 10% limit may be carried forward for 5 years. This is different from the higher AGI-based limits for individual taxpayers.

questions.question: A Section 754 election allows a partnership to:
options: Adjust the inside basis of partnership assets to reflect the purchasing partner's outside basis when a partnership interest is transferred or upon certain distributions
questions.explanation: Under IRC §754, a partnership may elect to adjust the basis of partnership property upon the transfer of a partnership interest (IRC §743(b) — optional basis adjustment) or upon a distribution of property (IRC §734(b)). The §743(b) adjustment links the inside basis of the partnership's assets to the new partner's outside basis (purchase price). Once made, the election applies to all future transfers and distributions until revoked (with IRS consent). The adjustment is allocable only to the transferee partner.

questions.question: A key advantage of organizing as a Limited Liability Company (LLC) for federal tax purposes is:
options: LLCs are always taxed as corporations
An LLC can choose its federal tax classification — sole proprietorship (single-member), partnership (multi-member), or corporation by checking the appropriate box on Form 8832
LLCs are exempt from all federal taxes
LLCs cannot be used for real estate investments
questions.explanation: Under the check-the-box regulations (Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3), a domestic LLC with a single member is disregarded by default (taxed as a sole proprietorship). An LLC with two or more members is treated as a partnership by default. Either type may elect to be classified as a corporation by filing Form 8832 (Entity Classification Election). An LLC that elects corporation status may then file Form 2553 to elect S corporation status. This flexibility is a major advantage.

questions.question: An S corporation election may be voluntarily revoked by shareholders holding more than:
options: 50% of the shares (both voting and nonvoting)
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(1), a corporation may revoke its S election with the consent of shareholders holding more than 50% of the outstanding shares of stock (both voting and nonvoting). If the revocation is made by March 15 of the tax year, it is effective for that year. If made after March 15, it is effective for the following year (unless a specific prospective date is specified). The revocation statement must be filed with the service center where the original election was filed.

questions.question: Under the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, the Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax (CAMT) applies to corporations with:
options: Average annual adjusted financial statement income exceeding $1 billion over a 3-year period
questions.explanation: The Corporate Alternative Minimum Tax (CAMT), enacted by the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022, imposes a 15% minimum tax on the adjusted financial statement income (book income) of applicable corporations — those with average annual adjusted financial statement income exceeding $1 billion over a consecutive 3-year period. The CAMT applies to tax years beginning after December 31, 2022. The tax is the excess of 15% of adjusted financial statement income over the regular corporate tax liability.

questions.question: In a partnership, recourse liabilities are allocated to:
options: The partner(s) who bear the economic risk of loss for the liability
Partners based on their profit-sharing ratio
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §1.752-2, a recourse liability is allocated to the partner(s) who bear the economic risk of loss (EROL). A partner has EROL to the extent they would be obligated to make a payment (and would not be entitled to reimbursement) if the partnership constructively liquidated — all liabilities became due, all assets became worthless, and the partnership wound up. In most general partnerships, general partners bear EROL for all recourse debt. In LLCs, EROL depends on the operating agreement.

questions.question: Which business entity provides the most favorable treatment for tax-free fringe benefits to owner-employees?
options: C corporation — shareholder-employees may receive tax-free fringe benefits such as health insurance, group term life insurance, and meals
S corporation — all benefits are tax-free
Partnership — partners may receive tax-free health insurance
questions.explanation: C corporations provide the most favorable fringe benefit treatment because shareholder-employees are treated the same as any other employee. Benefits such as employer-paid health insurance, group-term life insurance up to $50,000, dependent care assistance, and certain meals are tax-free to the shareholder-employee and deductible by the corporation. In S corporations and partnerships, more-than-2% shareholders/partners must include health insurance premiums in gross income (deductible on Schedule 1).

questions.question: An S corporation's election is automatically terminated if:
options: The corporation earns more than $1 million in revenue
The corporation ceases to meet eligibility requirements, such as exceeding 100 shareholders, having an ineligible shareholder, or having more than one class of stock
The corporation fails to file Form 1120-S by the due date
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(2), an S election is automatically terminated effective on the day the corporation ceases to be a small business corporation. Events that trigger termination include: exceeding 100 shareholders, having an ineligible shareholder (nonresident alien, corporation, most trusts, partnership), issuing a second class of stock, or having more than 25% of gross receipts from passive investment income for 3 consecutive years while having accumulated E&P. Inadvertent terminations may be corrected under IRC §1362(f).

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §1362(d)(2), an S election is automatically terminated effective on the day the corporation ceases to be a small business corporation.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation.

questions.question: Under current law (post-TCJA), a C corporation's net operating loss (NOL) may:
options: Be carried back 2 years and carried forward 20 years
Be carried forward indefinitely but limited to 80% of taxable income in the carryforward year
Be carried back 5 years with no limitation
questions.explanation: Under IRC §172 (as amended by the TCJA), NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2017 may only be carried forward indefinitely — no carryback is allowed (with limited exceptions for farming losses and certain insurance companies). The NOL deduction is limited to 80% of taxable income (computed without the NOL deduction) in the carryforward year. Pre-2018 NOLs continue to follow the prior 2-year carryback/20-year carryforward rules without the 80% limitation.

whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §172 (as amended by the TCJA), NOLs arising in tax years beginning after December 31, 2017 may only be carried forward indefinitely — no carryback is allowed (with limited exceptions for farming losses and certain insurance companies).

whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about c corporation.

questions.question: In a nonliquidating (current) distribution from a partnership to a partner, the partner generally:
options: Recognizes gain equal to the amount distributed
Recognizes gain only to the extent cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis; noncash property takes a carryover basis limited to the partner's remaining basis
questions.explanation: Under IRC §731(a), a partner generally does not recognize gain on a current distribution unless the cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis. No loss is recognized on a current distribution. Under IRC §732(a), distributed property (other than cash) takes the partnership's basis (carryover basis), but limited to the partner's remaining outside basis after reducing for cash received. The partner's outside basis is reduced by the amount of cash and the basis of property received.

whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §731(a), a partner generally does not recognize gain on a current distribution unless the cash received exceeds the partner's outside basis.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about partnership.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about partnership.

questions.question: An S corporation shareholder's loan basis (from loans made directly by the shareholder to the corporation) is:
options: Combined with stock basis for all purposes
A separate basis that may be reduced by losses only after stock basis reaches zero; any reduction must be restored by subsequent income before the shareholder may increase stock basis
Only relevant for gain calculations on sale
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1367 and Treas. Reg. §1.1367-2, a shareholder may deduct S corporation losses against loan basis after stock basis is reduced to zero. However, loan basis must be restored (by subsequent net income) before stock basis can be increased. Repayment of a loan that has been reduced results in capital gain to the shareholder. Importantly, only direct shareholder loans create debt basis — guaranteed loans or loans from related parties do not.

whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §1367 and Treas.
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation.
whyWrong.3: Why option D is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about s corporation.

questions.question: An S corporation must generally use which tax year?
options: Any fiscal year chosen by the shareholders
A calendar year ending December 31, unless a valid business purpose is established or a Section 444 election is made
The same tax year as its largest shareholder
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1378, an S corporation must use the calendar year (ending December 31) as its tax year, unless it can establish a business purpose for a different year to the satisfaction of the IRS, or it makes a Section 444 election (which allows a fiscal year ending September, October, or November with a required payment). The required year restriction prevents deferral of income to shareholders.

questions.question: A distribution from a C corporation to its shareholders is treated as a dividend to the extent of:
options: The corporation's current and accumulated earnings and profits (E&P)
The shareholder's basis in the stock
questions.explanation: Under IRC §316, a dividend is any distribution of property from a corporation to its shareholders out of either current earnings and profits (current year E&P) or accumulated earnings and profits (E&P from prior years). Distributions are sourced first from current E&P, then from accumulated E&P. Distributions exceeding total E&P are treated as return of capital (reducing stock basis), and amounts exceeding basis are treated as capital gain.

whyWrong.0: Why option A is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about c corporation.
whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §316, a dividend is any distribution of property from a corporation to its shareholders out of either current earnings and profits (current year E&P) or accumulated earnings and profits (E&P from prior years).
whyWrong.2: Why option C is WRONG - This does not correctly address the question about c corporation.

questions.question: Under current law (post-TCJA), a partnership terminates for tax purposes when:
options: 50% or more of partnership interests are transferred within 12 months
No part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any of its partners
Two or more partners disagree on operations
questions.explanation: Under the TCJA amendments to IRC §708(b), the "technical termination" rule (when 50% or more of interests were transferred in 12 months) was repealed for partnership tax years beginning after December 31, 2017. Now, a partnership terminates for tax purposes only when no part of any business, financial operation, or venture of the partnership continues to be carried on by any partner in a partnership. This simplification eliminated many unintended termination consequences.

questions.question: An S corporation with accumulated E&P from C corporation years faces what consequence if its passive investment income exceeds 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive years?
options: Nothing — S corporations are never affected by passive income
The S corporation election is automatically terminated, and the corporation reverts to C corporation status
The corporation must pay a special penalty tax
The shareholders must pay an additional 10% surtax
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1362(d)(3), if an S corporation has accumulated E&P (from prior C corporation years) and passive investment income (rents, royalties, dividends, interest, annuities, gains from sales of securities) exceeding 25% of gross receipts for three consecutive tax years, the S election is automatically terminated at the beginning of the fourth year. Additionally, under IRC §1375, an excess net passive income tax (at the 21% corporate rate) is imposed for each year that the passive income exceeds 25% of gross receipts while there is accumulated E&P.

questions.question: Under IRC §704(e), a family member who receives a partnership interest by gift:
options: Is treated the same as any other partner with no special rules
Is recognized as a partner only if capital is a material income-producing factor; the donor must be allocated reasonable compensation for services before income is allocated to the donee
Cannot be a partner under any circumstances
Must contribute cash equal to the gifted interest's value
questions.explanation: Under IRC §704(e), family partnerships are subject to special rules. A donee family member is recognized as a partner if capital is a material income-producing factor and the donee has genuine ownership and control. The donor-partner must be allocated reasonable compensation for services rendered to the partnership before allocating income between the partners. The donee's share of income cannot exceed the proportionate share based on the donated capital interest.

whyWrong.1: Why option B is CORRECT - Under IRC §704(e), family partnerships are subject to special rules.

questions.question: Under IRC §368(a), which of the following is a Type A reorganization?
options: A statutory merger or consolidation under state law
A transfer of assets for stock in a newly formed corporation
A simple purchase of a corporation's assets for cash
questions.explanation: Under IRC §368(a)(1)(A), a Type A reorganization is a statutory merger or consolidation effected under applicable state law (or federal law or the law of a foreign jurisdiction). In a merger, one corporation absorbs another; in a consolidation, two corporations combine to form a new entity. Key requirements include continuity of interest (significant portion of consideration must be stock) and continuity of business enterprise. Type A reorganizations offer the most flexibility in consideration types.

questions.question: When a C corporation completely liquidates under IRC §331, shareholders generally:
options: Report the distribution as ordinary income
Treat the liquidating distribution as full payment in exchange for their stock, recognizing capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the distribution amount and their stock basis
Do not recognize any gain or loss
Report the distribution as a dividend
questions.explanation: Under IRC §331, amounts received by a shareholder in a complete liquidation of a corporation are treated as full payment in exchange for the stock. The shareholder recognizes capital gain or loss equal to the difference between the amount received (FMV of property plus cash) and the shareholder's adjusted basis in the stock. Under IRC §336, the liquidating corporation recognizes gain or loss on the distribution of property as if it sold the property at FMV.

questions.question: Under IRC §332, a parent corporation that liquidates an 80%-or-more owned subsidiary:
options: Recognizes gain on the received assets
Recognizes no gain or loss; the subsidiary's asset bases carry over to the parent
Must pay a special liquidation tax
Recognizes loss on the received assets
questions.explanation: Under IRC §332, when a parent corporation (owning 80% or more of the subsidiary's stock) liquidates the subsidiary, the parent recognizes no gain or loss. Under IRC §334(b)(1), the parent takes a carryover basis in the subsidiary's assets. The subsidiary also recognizes no gain or loss on distributions to the 80% parent under IRC §337. However, the subsidiary does recognize gain (not loss) on distributions to minority shareholders. The parent also inherits the subsidiary's tax attributes (NOLs, E&P, etc.) under IRC §381.

questions.question: When a C corporation elects S corporation status, which of the following tax consequences should be considered?
options: All accumulated E&P is immediately distributed and taxed
Built-in gains tax on appreciated assets, LIFO recapture tax, passive investment income tax on accumulated E&P, and accounting method changes
No tax consequences — the conversion is seamless
The corporation must liquidate and reform
questions.explanation: Converting from C to S status triggers several considerations: (1) the built-in gains (BIG) tax under IRC §1374 on net unrealized appreciation within 5 years; (2) LIFO recapture — if the corporation used LIFO inventory, it must include the LIFO reserve in income in the last C corporation year (IRC §1363(d)); (3) excess passive income tax under IRC §1375 if passive income exceeds 25% of gross receipts while accumulated E&P exists; and (4) potential accounting method changes. Planning these issues is critical.

questions.question: In a liquidating distribution from a partnership, a departing partner may recognize a loss only if:
options: The partnership has an overall loss for the year
The only property distributed is cash, unrealized receivables, and/or inventory, and the total is less than the partner's outside basis
The partner has held the interest for more than 5 years
The partnership files a special election
questions.explanation: Under IRC §731(a)(2), a partner may recognize a loss on a liquidating distribution only if the distribution consists solely of cash, unrealized receivables, and/or inventory items, and the total amount of cash plus the basis of the receivables and inventory is less than the partner's outside basis. The loss is a capital loss. If any other property is distributed, no loss may be recognized — instead, the partner takes a substituted basis in the property.

questions.question: For a stock redemption to qualify for sale or exchange treatment (capital gain) rather than dividend treatment, it must meet one of the tests under IRC §302(b). Which of the following qualifies?
options: Any repurchase of stock by the corporation
A substantially disproportionate redemption (shareholder's ownership drops below 80% of pre-redemption percentage and below 50% total voting power)
A redemption that increases the shareholder's ownership percentage
questions.explanation: Under IRC §302(b)(2), a substantially disproportionate redemption qualifies for sale/exchange treatment if, after the redemption: (1) the shareholder owns less than 50% of the total combined voting power, and (2) the shareholder's percentage of voting stock is less than 80% of their pre-redemption percentage. Other qualifying tests include: a complete termination of interest (§302(b)(3)), a redemption not essentially equivalent to a dividend (§302(b)(1)), or a partial liquidation (§302(b)(4)).

questions.question: The IRS may recharacterize S corporation distributions as wages if:
options: The shareholder-employee receives distributions but little or no salary, and the salary does not reflect reasonable compensation for services performed
The corporation has more than $500,000 in revenue
The shareholder owns less than 50% of the stock
questions.explanation: The IRS scrutinizes S corporations where shareholder-employees receive minimal or no salary while taking significant distributions. Under IRC §7436, the IRS may recharacterize distributions as wages subject to FICA and income tax withholding if the compensation is not reasonable for the services provided. Factors considered include: training and experience, duties and responsibilities, comparable salaries, time devoted, dividend history, and compensation agreements.

questions.question: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B), a transaction between a partner and a partnership may be recharacterized as a disguised sale if:
options: The partner contributes property and receives an equal value distribution within 2 years, and the distribution would not have been made but for the contribution
The partner receives any guaranteed payment
The partner borrows money from the partnership
questions.explanation: Under IRC §707(a)(2)(B) and Treas. Reg. §1.707-3, a contribution of property by a partner followed by a related distribution from the partnership (or vice versa) may be treated as a disguised sale if, viewed together, the transfer and distribution are properly characterized as a sale. If a distribution occurs within 2 years of a contribution, there is a presumption of a disguised sale. Factors include: the timing and amount of the distribution, the entrepreneurial risks borne, and whether the distribution would have occurred regardless.

questions.question: Under IRC §1202, a noncorporate taxpayer may exclude from gross income what percentage of gain on the sale of Qualified Small Business Stock (QSBS) acquired after September 27, 2010 and held for more than 5 years?
options: 100%, up to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the adjusted basis of the stock
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1202, a noncorporate taxpayer may exclude 100% of the gain from the sale of QSBS acquired after September 27, 2010 (75% for stock acquired between February 18, 2009 and September 27, 2010; 50% for stock acquired before that). The exclusion is limited to the greater of $10 million or 10 times the taxpayer's adjusted basis in the stock. QSBS requirements include: (1) the stock must be in a C corporation with gross assets not exceeding $50 million, (2) acquired at original issuance, and (3) held for more than 5 years.

questions.question: The Section 199A Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction is available to owners of which types of pass-through entities?
options: S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates with qualified business income
questions.explanation: The Section 199A deduction is available to noncorporate taxpayers with qualified business income from pass-through entities, including S corporations, partnerships, sole proprietorships, and certain trusts and estates. The deduction is generally 20% of QBI, subject to limitations based on taxable income, W-2 wages paid, and the unadjusted basis of qualified property. C corporations are not eligible because they are taxed at the entity level at 21%.

questions.question: For a more-than-2% shareholder of an S corporation, employer-paid health insurance premiums are:
options: Included in the shareholder's W-2 wages as income (subject to income tax but not FICA), then deductible by the shareholder on Schedule 1 of Form 1040
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1372, a more-than-2% S corporation shareholder is treated like a partner for purposes of fringe benefits. Health insurance premiums paid by the S corporation must be included in the shareholder-employee's W-2 wages (Box 1 only — not subject to FICA). The shareholder may then claim the self-employed health insurance deduction on Line 17 of Schedule 1 (Form 1040), which is an above-the-line deduction. This effectively makes the premium deductible but through a different mechanism than for C corporation shareholder-employees.

questions.question: Under the at-risk rules of IRC §465, a partner's deductible losses are limited to:
options: The amount the partner has at risk — generally cash contributions, adjusted basis of property contributed, and amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable or has pledged property other than used in the activity
questions.explanation: Under IRC §465, a partner may only deduct losses to the extent of the amount they have "at risk" in the activity. The at-risk amount includes: (1) cash contributed, (2) the adjusted basis of property contributed, (3) amounts borrowed for which the partner is personally liable, and (4) amounts borrowed secured by property not used in the activity. Nonrecourse loans generally do NOT increase the at-risk amount (except for qualified nonrecourse financing for real estate under IRC §465(b)(6)).

questions.question: A C corporation that receives dividends from another domestic corporation is entitled to a dividends received deduction (DRD) of:
options: 50% for less than 20% ownership, 65% for 20-79% ownership, or 100% for 80% or more ownership
questions.explanation: Under IRC §243, the DRD for dividends received from another domestic corporation is: 50% if the recipient owns less than 20% of the paying corporation (after TCJA, reduced from 70%); 65% if ownership is 20% or more but less than 80% (reduced from 80%); and 100% if ownership is 80% or more (affiliated group). The DRD may be limited to a percentage of taxable income. The DRD mitigates triple taxation of corporate earnings.

questions.question: A single-member LLC that has not elected corporate classification is treated for federal tax purposes as:
options: A disregarded entity — its income and expenses are reported on the owner's individual return (Schedule C for business activities)
questions.explanation: Under Treas. Reg. §301.7701-3(b)(1)(ii), a domestic eligible entity with a single owner that does not file Form 8832 to elect corporate treatment is disregarded as an entity separate from its owner. Its business income and expenses are reported directly on the owner's tax return (Schedule C for business operations, Schedule E for rental activities). However, the SMLLC may still be required to file certain forms (employment tax returns, excise tax returns) under its own EIN.

questions.question: Under the "economic effect" prong of the substantial economic effect test, allocations must satisfy all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Allocations must be reflected in capital accounts maintained in accordance with Treasury Regulations
Liquidating distributions must be made in accordance with positive capital account balances
Partners must agree to restore any deficit capital account balance upon liquidation (or the allocation must meet the alternate test with a qualified income offset)
Allocations must be proportional to partner ownership percentages
questions.explanation: The economic effect test under Treas. Reg. §1.704-1(b)(2)(ii) requires: (1) capital accounts are maintained in accordance with the regulations; (2) upon liquidation, distributions are made in accordance with positive capital account balances; and (3) partners with deficit capital accounts must restore the deficit upon liquidation (or the allocation must satisfy the alternate test with a qualified income offset). The test does NOT require that allocations be proportional to ownership — the entire purpose of the rules is to allow non-proportional allocations.

whyWrong.3: Why option D is CORRECT - The economic effect test under Treas.

questions.question: Which of the following may NOT be a shareholder of an S corporation?
options: An estate or certain qualifying trusts
A single-member LLC owned by a U.S. resident
questions.explanation: Under IRC §1361(b)(1)(B), eligible S corporation shareholders include: U.S. citizen and resident alien individuals, estates, certain trusts (grantor trusts, QSSTs, ESBTs, testamentary trusts), and tax-exempt organizations under IRC §501(c)(3) and §501(a). Ineligible shareholders include: partnerships, corporations, nonresident aliens, and most other types of trusts. A single-member LLC owned by an individual is a disregarded entity, so the individual is treated as the shareholder.
